content.assessment: This proposal operates on segregation drivers independent of the tracking mechanism itself, so it does not directly change students' exposure to the selection/ranking psychology inherent in the 6-/8-year track system. Its likely psychological effects operate through composition — changing who attends which school without changing the fact that schools remain differentiated by track and performance level. This could reduce some geographic/demographic drivers of stratified peer exposure that interact with self-concept and aspiration, but the core reference-group and ranking-related mechanisms tied to academic selection remain unaddressed.
opportunities: Reducing geographic/choice-driven stratification could marginally narrow the achievement-range gap between school types, potentially softening some reference-group effects, though this pathway is unquantified in the evidence provided.
strengths: Addressing choice/commuting-driven stratification could reduce some peer-composition extremes that interact with reference-group effects, independent of track-age reform.
Does not require altering the age-10 selection point, so it avoids reopening politically volatile timing debates while still targeting a distinct segregation channel.
threats: Legal and administrative reversal risk (as with prior desegregation stagnation and Curia exemption precedent) could limit durability of any compositional shift and its psychological correlates.
Constrained local planning capacity under centralization may prevent zoning changes from meaningfully altering peer composition, limiting any indirect psychological benefit.
weaknesses: Does not address the core selection/ranking psychology (performance orientation, fear of failure) associated with the entrance-exam-based track system itself.
No direct mechanism to reduce big-fish-little-pond costs experienced by students already sorted into differentiated tracks.
